name: spec-driven-development
reasoning: A tight 6-step spec-driven development pipeline (spec → criteria → rules → review → tasks → execute) authored by Anton Arhipov in his dedicated skills collection (13 stars). Every step explicitly declares its pipeline position and reads the previous step's output (spec/proposal.md → spec/spec.md → spec/criteria.md → spec/rules.md → spec/review.md → spec/tasks.yaml), forming a single indivisible suite. Individual skill files are 79–228 lines with rich, opinionated guidance — EARS acceptance-criteria templates with traceability IDs, RFC-2119 constraint language, a severity-graded review protocol, YAML task-list schema, and a full execution-loop with Checkpoint/Blocker report formats. Per rule 5, a coherent, high-quality workflow bundle is directory-worthy under the workflow category; this one easily meets that bar and is the canonical copy (highest stars, most steps, deepest content) per rule 2.

name: spec-driven-development-workshop
reasoning: A 5-step workshop/teaching variant of the same SDD pipeline (clarify → analyze → constrain → validate → generate-plan), thinner per member (36–83 lines each vs 79–228 lines in the canonical) and hosted in a workshop repo (2 stars vs 13). The pipeline is cohesive — each step reads the previous step's artifact — but this is clearly a teaching/conference variant of the canonical found in agentskills. Per the Devnexus 2026 rule, the canonical multi-step pipeline (agentskills, 6 steps, richer content) is promoted as found; this workshop variant is needs_review at best and should not become a second found listing for the same suite.

name: spec-driven-dev-pipeline
reasoning: Six numbered SKILL.md files form one end-to-end spec-driven development pipeline (proposal → spec → criteria → rules → review → tasks → execute). Each step reads files produced by its predecessor and makes no sense in isolation — a textbook Rule 4 bundle. Total content is ~1040 lines of substantive, language-agnostic guidance: EARS-based acceptance criteria with B-N → AC-N → RULE-N traceability, an Ecosystem Survey gate for technology decisions, a severity-tagged cross-document review step that produces a PASS/FAIL verdict gating the task step, YAML task output with dependency graph and checkpoints, and a checkpoint-halting execution loop. No JVM-specific content, but the Rule 5 escape applies — this is a coherent, high-quality workflow bundle, not a thin single step. The host repo (junie-midi-javafx-app, 0 stars) is an app project, but Rule 1 bars that as a reject signal; the skills make zero reference to JavaFX or MIDI and are fully reusable.
